metadata.title: Quick-fire: When you're allocating something that isn't yours
metadata.author: patterns-of-choice editorial draft
metadata.design_intent: Fifth resource-allocation quick-fire. Where the prior four varied counterparty (qf-001), resource-shape (qf-003), resource-type (qf-005), and help-structure (qf-007) — all in contexts where the user allocates their own resources — this round shifts to institutional allocation: voting on funds, board-level distributional choices, finite-budget cuts, public credit, lottery participation. The construct of interest is what happens when the user has fiduciary or structural responsibility for distributing resources that aren't strictly theirs.
metadata.domain_anchor: Procedural-justice literature (Leventhal, Thibaut & Walker) for the institutional-fairness contexts. Konow 1996 'A Positive Theory of Economic Fairness' on fiduciary allocation. Engel 2011 dictator-meta still informs the base rates but the construct asymmetry is real.

items.prompt: You're on a small committee voting on which of two local programs gets renewed funding. You have private information (a friend works in one) suggesting Program A is more effective; the committee mostly believes Program B is.

options.text: Argue for A using the private information explicitly: 'I should disclose, but...'
Vote with the committee's reading. Your private information is too entangled to disclose cleanly.

items.prompt: You're on a small nonprofit board choosing between two grants you can fund. One helps a smaller number of people significantly; the other helps a larger number moderately. Both meet the org's mission.

options.text: Argue for the smaller-deeper option. Significant change in fewer lives is preferable to thin help.
Argue for the larger-thinner option. Reach matters; broader impact is what scale is for.

items.prompt: Your team budget has been cut. Two ongoing projects need to share what was previously one project's funding. Your closer collaborators run Project A; you respect Project B's lead but don't know them well.

options.text: Propose 50/50 cuts to both projects. The strict-fairness move neutralizes the relationship asymmetry.
Propose proportional cuts based on each project's measured impact. Acknowledge the relationship-bias risk and try to control for it.

items.prompt: You helped draft a relative's will. The will leaves you a share substantially larger than seems proportional to your role in their life. They're not gone yet but the discrepancy is on your mind.

options.text: Raise it with them directly: 'I notice the share you've given me feels larger than I'd have expected. Can we talk about it?'
Accept it as their decision. They had the right to allocate as they chose; questioning it could feel like rejection.

items.prompt: A lottery for limited-availability tickets to a popular event is being held among a friend group. Five tickets, twelve interested people. You don't particularly want to go but don't dislike the idea either.

options.text: Participate; tickets are zero-sum and you might be the right person for one.
Withdraw to improve someone else's odds; you don't need it as much as some friends will.

items.prompt: Your team won a public-facing award; the writeup names you as the lead. The truth is one of your direct reports did most of the meaningful work. The org wants the writeup published as is.

options.text: Push back on the org: 'The writeup should name [report] as a co-lead. Here's why specifically.'
Accept the framing. The org's narrative has its own logic; the report can be acknowledged in other ways.
